medical_history.chief_complaint: chronic facial pain and ptosis on her left side
medical_history.history_of_present_illness: The patient reported suffering from a burning constant pain above the left eyebrow for about 10 years, which was sometimes accompanied by feelings of heat, redness, and tingling. The pain suddenly stopped three weeks before admission, leaving only a slight left-sided facial hypaesthesia.
medical_history.past_medical_history: Hypertension, glaucoma, and iron deficiency. The patient had undergone several MRI scans and treatments for suspected trigeminal neuralgia, migraine, and cluster headache without success.
medical_history.social_history: Married, mother of a daughter, and professionally successful as a businesswoman. No mention of substance use or occupational exposures.

physical_examination.specialty_examination: Left-sided mild ptosis and hypaesthesia and disturbance of thermoceception in the entire left trigeminal nerve area. The rest of the neurological and general clinical status was normal.

laboratory_examinations.procedure_name: Serology for Varizella Zoster and Borreliosis
laboratory_examinations.findings: No association with infection or autoimmune disease
Unremarkable in terms of laboratory chemistry and cytology

radiographic_examinations.findings: Initially, no morphological correlate found. Later, a 2 mm signal alteration in the caudal part of the left trigeminal nucleus was noted. Finally, a 5–6 mm subacute hemorrhaged cavernoma was detected.
radiographic_examinations.changes_over_time: Signal alteration was constant over the years. Later, a subacute hemorrhage into a small brainstem cavernoma was found.

other_tests.findings: Conduction delay on the left side (R1r, 8.8 ms, R2r 29.8 ms, R2’l 28.8 ms, stimulation left: R1 l 8.7 ms, R2 l 32.7 ms, R2’r 31.0 ms)
Delayed and less pronounced on the left side (right 18.9 ms, left 19.9 ms)

diagnosis.diagnostic_reasoning: The final diagnosis was supported by MRI findings showing a subacute hemorrhage into a small brainstem cavernoma. The patient's chronic facial pain and subsequent hypesthesia were attributed to the cavernoma affecting the trigeminal nerve nucleus. Previous diagnostic attempts under various suspected conditions (Trigeminal neuralgia, Migraine, Cluster headache, Erythroprosopalgia) were unsuccessful, and the cavernoma was only identified upon reevaluation of MRI images with specific sequences.
